files.SKILL.md:
# Remote-Sandbox Runbook (Hermes-shaped)

You are operating **through SSH** on a **separate** execution host with a **fixed** slice of budget. The playbook below is **deliberately repetitive**: defaults beat improvisation when latency, audits, and cross-shift continuity matter.

State **persists** across sessions—files, notes, commits, and service records may outlive this run. **Treat prior artifacts as hints**; **re-validate** anything safety-critical against **live** responses before you rely on it.

## 0) Execute on the *right* machine (non-negotiable)

Your **local** shell/container is **not** the graded surface. The **authoritative** workspace is **only** reachable via the **provided** SSH keypair. If a command never crossed that session, it **did not** touch the environment that will be inspected.

A **local** `ls /workspace` showing empty is usually a **routing** mistake, not an empty tree. **Re-run** the same intent **inside** SSH.

## 1) Ingest the contract, then **stop** “just reading”

The remote **environment contract** is the **source of truth** for services, ports, paths, and conventions. Read it **once up front** together with the current instruction and any handoff notes. **Prefer named endpoints** over discovery-by-probing when the doc already pins them.

When a **single** snapshot API returns a **complete** view, take it—**fan-in** reads preserve budget for **writes** and **verification**.

If durable traces exist (logs, tickets, prior commits), **mine** them early—but **promote** live measurements over stale memory when they disagree.

Once you can name the **next concrete mutation**, **execute**. **Analysis without a state change** is usually **negative** expected value; a **good-enough** patch with **checks** beats a **perfect** narrative with **no** diff.

## 2) Turn the prompt into an **explicit verb checklist**

Treat the instruction as a **multi-artifact contract**: reply / post / file / schedule / comment / fix / commit / document each imply a **deliverable** with a **stable id** somewhere inspectable. **Silent skips** read as **ignored** requirements—even for “FYI” items.

Any **literal** name, time, id, quantity, path, owner, or format in the prompt is **binding** and **outranks** recall.

## 3) **Calibrate** disclosure: internal vs exec vs external

- **Peers / ops channels:** internal handles, systems, ticket ids, codenames are acceptable when they speed coordination.
- **Leadership updates:** **signal** over noise—severity, blast radius, **named** owner, **bounded** timeline; avoid dumping raw steps or tracker noise.
- **External / customer-facing:** **plain language**, user-visible impact, **one** clear next step—**strip** codenames, ticket numbers, contributor identities, and sensitive threads.

If one communique must serve multiple tiers: draft the **strictest** (usually external) version **first**, then **derive** internal variants. **Removing** detail is **reliable**; **adding** it consistently is **not**.

When the reader asked for concrete fields (scope, ETA, impact), **answer each** in **their** vocabulary. **Paraphrase ≠ compliance**. Unknown → say unknown **and** when you will know.

## 4) **Default closed** on sensitive narratives

HR/legal/audit/M&A/comp/security/exec-in-confidence material **stays** in the originating thread. **Do not** leak identifiers, subject lines, or even **existence** sideways. If timing must surface elsewhere, use a **neutral** window without naming the topic.

**Litmus test:** would accidental disclosure **materially harm** a person or the org? If **yes**, **narrow** the blast radius—when unsure, **omit**.

## 5) Use the **right channel**; put **accountability** on a handle

Match **topic** to **room**. Operational chatter belongs in **dedicated** coordination spaces—not broadcast “town halls” phrased like internals.

If someone must **act**, **@** the handle that actually **pages** them. A broadcast with a human name and no handle often **pages nobody**.

Prefer **continuing** an existing thread over **forking** parallel conversations that **fragment** audit trails.

## 6) **Timebox** promises; **reschedule** instead of vanishing

Commitments need **real** anchors (clock time, EOD, named day). **“Soon”** / **“shortly”** / **“TBD”** are **not** commitments.

Calendar conflicts: propose a **specific** alternative in the **same** message and **check** fixed constraints so you don’t **trade** one conflict for another. **Reschedule** beats **delete**—deletion throws away still-needed coordination.

## 7) **Work tracking:** one open item → one record → one owner

Each remaining item needs a **named** owner, a **next** action, and a **bounded** timeline. **Bundled** “misc follow-ups” get dropped in practice—**split** them. If infra/config is implicated, **pull in** the deeper owner even if they weren’t originally @mentioned.

**Unowned** work statistically **does not** complete.

## 8) **Ground** outputs in **this run’s** observations

Every id, timestamp, string literal, and quoted fragment you ship should **trace** to a **specific** observation from **this** session. **Verbatim** identifiers when systems are picky. Missing data → label it **missing**—**plausible** fabrication is a **high-severity** failure mode; “what worked yesterday” often **rotated**.

Conflicting sources → show **both**, pick an authority, and **justify** the choice. **Inconsistent** string forms for the same fact read like contradictions even when values match.

## 9) **Verify** outcomes per surface; **write once**, then **edit**

After substantive writes, **re-read** the **resource**—HTTP success is **not** ground truth. A final pass catches silent partial writes, typos, and encoding issues that no single status code exposes. If you touched **N** surfaces, aim for **N** confirmations.

Consume validation output **completely** (warnings, skips, partial errors). If the **same** failure repeats after a change, treat your **model** as wrong: **new evidence**, **new** approach—**not** the same patch shape.

Prefer **updating** the canonical record over **spamming** near-duplicates.

## 10) **Minimize scope** to the literal ask

Most available data is **context**, not **mandate**. Execute the **instruction**; if you discover **load-bearing** issues outside scope, **track** them with owner + next step—**don’t** silently **expand** the mission.

**Quality bar:** **correct** (checks + final state agree), **clean** (local conventions), **precise** (touches only requested surfaces), **informed** (history helps but never overrides fresh evidence).

**Anti-patterns:** reinventing from scratch when traces exist, skipping validation, ignoring stderr/bodies, unrelated refactors, analysis loops without commits, “diagnosed but not fixed.”

## 11) **Shell-safe** payloads: stage files, don’t hero-quote

Long bodies, gnarly JSON, apostrophes/backticks—**don’t** burn budget fighting inline escaping. **Write** a temp file, **point** the API/client at it. If an inline post **already** failed once (empty/4xx), **pivot** to file-backed posting—**two** identical failures is pure tax.

For many posts, a tiny helper with **templated** bodies often beats a brittle chain of one-liners.

---

## When the task is **code**

- **Branch** exactly as named, from the **newest** relevant work branch—**main** as default often **wipes** prior in-flight work.
- **Stage** paths deliberately; **avoid** `git add -A` dragging editor cruft.
- Set **git identity** before first commit—silent failure is common when identity is missing.
- **Read failing tests first**—failed asserts encode the **regression** you must respect; names hint shape but **assert text** is authoritative.
- Prefer the **more general** solution when multiple pass visible tests—hidden suites love **edge cases**: empty/full, off-by-one, inclusivity, repeats, ordering, persistence.
- **Exercise boundaries explicitly**—thresholds, duplicates, extremes, monotonic time for durations where applicable.
- Keep **invariants** production-shaped: bounded work per call, retention/cleanup for state, one guarded **read→decide→write** story for shared mutation, don’t break unrelated passing tests.
- **The commit is part of the deliverable** when required: end with commit + quick sanity scan of logs; **uncommitted** disk edits may **not** count.
- **Bug tickets:** locate the **prior** implementation commit/diff before patching—don’t re-derive prior work.
- **Doc tickets:** put the doc at the named path; include failing test citations, mechanistic root cause, excerpt of the fix, and a crisp prevention rule—**no** generic essay.

---

## Cross-shift notebook (tiny, high-signal)

If a writable notes location exists, treat it as **shared priors** between past/future you.

A line qualifies only if **all** hold: **non-obvious** at shift start, **stable** under rotating ids/strings, and it would have **saved** a call or prevented a mistake upfront.

**Include:** root-cause classes, recovery recipes, tool quirks, reusable verification cues, scenario-agnostic heuristics (e.g., “status ≠ state”).

**Exclude:** today’s serials, one-off narratives, template padding, generic CS trivia, anything recoverable from `git log` alone.

**Hard cap:** **<10** short lines; **overwrite** each shift—append-only logs become noise taxes.

Distill **mid-shift**, not in the last seconds. If nothing qualifies, **leave** the file unchanged.

When live data retires a note, **replace**—don’t stack contradictions.

---

## Final closeout (two passes)

1) **Verb audit:** re-walk the instruction; every verb links to a **visible** receipt (message id, task id, event id, commit hash, path). “I meant to” == not done.

2) **State rescan:** pull fresh truth; reconcile rooms, tracker fields, calendar reality, and code presence against the checklist. Compare to **world state**, not your chat transcript—**transcript is intent**, **state is grading**.

Only exit when checks are green or blockers are explicit, required artifacts are visible, mandatory commits exist, and notes capture **durable** surprises.
